commit.message:
refactor: projects + conversation schema unification (#198)

* refactor: projects + conversation schema unification (#196)

Renames graph schema and codebase to the unified ontology:

Graph schema:
- Parachute_Session → Conversation
- Parachute_ContainerEnv → Project (adds core_memory field)
- Chat_Session dropped (merged into Conversation)
- Chat_Exchange → Exchange
- Journal_Entry → Note (adds note_type, aliases, status, created_by)
- Day, HAS_ENTRY, HAS_CARD dropped (query Note by date field)

Server (computer/):
- migrate_schema_v2() handles one-time graph migration at startup
- /api/containers → /api/projects, container_env_id → project_id
- Project core_memory injected as ## Project Context in system prompt
- Sessions endpoint: default filters to human-initiated sessions; ?all=true shows all

Flutter (app/):
- ContainerEnv model → Project (coreMemory field added)
- container_env_providers → project_providers
- container_env_service → project_service
- Brain tab: Parachute_ContainerEnv → Project, Journal_Entry → Note
- currentSessionProjectIdProvider replaces currentSessionContainerEnvIdProvider
- Fixed pre-existing missing import for remoteCurrentPathProvider in main.dart

Tests:
- test_container_files.py: get_container_env → get_project
- test_orchestrator_phases.py: container_env_id → project_id fixtures
- test_daily_module.py: Journal_Entry → Note, remove Day table test

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* refactor: rename Conversation graph table to Chat

Simpler, more direct name for the session node table.
Chat → Chat (graph table), matching the chat feature name.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix(migration): address review findings from PR #198

Critical fixes:
- Create Exchange table/HAS_EXCHANGE rel inside migrate_schema_v2 before
  migrating data — modules haven't loaded yet at migration time, so Exchange
  didn't exist and all exchange data was silently lost
- Drop HAS_ENTRY, HAS_CARD rel tables before dropping Journal_Entry/Day node
  tables — Kuzu requires rel tables dropped first
- Drop old HAS_EXCHANGE rel table before dropping Parachute_Session (prevents
  migration looping on restart if DROP TABLE fails)
- Add missing fields to Journal_Entry→Note migration: snippet, brain_links_json,
  metadata_json, entry_type (brain_links_json in particular is user data)
- Hold write_lock around all graph writes in migration

Other fixes:
- mcp_server.py list_projects tool: /container_envs → /projects (was 404)
- models/session.py: enforce core_memory max_length=4000 in Pydantic (not just
  a comment), applies to both Project and ProjectCreate
- Delete orphaned legacy files: containers.py, container_env_providers.dart,
  container_env_service.dart, container_env.dart (zero callers, dead code)
- brain_home_screen.dart _previewKeys: update 'Parachute_Session'→'Chat',
  'Journal_Entry'→'Note' (was hitting default case, wrong preview columns)
- Fix stale docstrings in graph_sessions.py and graph.py

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix(migration): delete rel table edges before DROP TABLE

Kuzu requires rel tables to be empty before DROP. The old HAS_EXCHANGE
edges (Chat_Session→Chat_Exchange) prevented the table from being dropped,
which then prevented the new HAS_EXCHANGE (Chat→Exchange) from being created
by ensure_rel_table. Same fix applied to HAS_ENTRY/HAS_CARD drops.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* refactor: remove completed migration code

All migrations (vault restructure, SQLite→Graph, schema v2 rename) have
run successfully. Remove migration.py and startup calls to prevent
re-importing cleared data on every server restart.

ephemeral so Anthropic caches it server-side for 5 minutes. Dynamic

system prompt expands with examples or additional instructions.

contribute per-aircraft feature toggles that appear in the Settings tab
alongside built-in features. Each feature has a name slug, label, and
description.

Built-in features are dog-fooded through the same registry path:

instead of maintaining a hardcoded KNOWN_FEATURES list in models.py.

- core/plugins.py: add aircraft_features class attr, register_features(),
  feature_catalog and known_feature_names properties on PluginRegistry
- core/features.py: add BUILTIN_FEATURE_CATALOG, get_feature_catalog(),
  get_known_feature_names(); remove KNOWN_FEATURES from models.py
- core/apps.py: register builtin features in CoreConfig.ready()
- API responses (summary, features action, public view) now include
  feature_catalog alongside features for frontend consumption
- Alpine.js: featureCatalog state populated from API; featureLabel() and

chore: upgrade hypha-rpc to 0.21.32 and bump hypha to 0.21.72 (#921)

* perf: remove gc.get_objects() and gc.collect() from disconnect, add punsubscribe timeout

Critical performance fix for high-scale deployments (100K+ concurrent users):

1. Remove gc.get_objects() from RedisRPCConnection.disconnect()
   - Was scanning ALL Python objects (O(n_total)) on every disconnect
   - At scale, each disconnect scanned millions of objects causing severe GC pressure
   - RPC cleanup is correctly handled via the _handle_disconnected callback chain

2. Remove gc.collect() called every 20 disconnects
   - Manual GC collection disrupts Python's automatic GC tuning
   - Creates latency spikes during high-throughput disconnect periods

3. Add asyncio.wait_for(timeout=5.0) to punsubscribe calls
   - unsubscribe_from_client_events() and cleanup_orphaned_patterns() now timeout
   - Prevents disconnect from hanging indefinitely when Redis is slow/unavailable

4. Add test_disconnect_does_not_use_gc_get_objects to verify the fix

Add Known Performance Anti-Patterns section to prevent future regressions:
- gc.get_objects() in hot paths is O(n_total_objects)
- gc.collect() in disconnect disrupts Python GC tuning
- redis.keys() blocks entire Redis - use SCAN instead
- Missing timeouts on Redis cleanup operations

* perf: replace blocking redis.keys() with non-blocking redis.scan() in workspace

redis.keys(pattern) is O(n) over ALL Redis keys and blocks the entire Redis
server for the duration of the scan. With 100K+ service entries this causes
seconds-long stalls that affect all concurrent users.

Fix _list_client_keys() and ping_client() fallback in workspace.py:
- Replace `await redis.keys(pattern)` with cursor-based scan loop
- Use count=500 batches to yield control between iterations
- Handle both bytes and str key formats consistently

Also add tests/test_scan_vs_keys.py:

Replaces 9 remaining blocking redis.keys() calls with a new _scan_keys()
helper that uses cursor-based SCAN iteration.

Each redis.keys() call blocks the entire Redis server for its full duration
— O(n_total_keys). With 100K+ service entries this can stall all concurrent
operations for hundreds of milliseconds.

- Add WorkspaceManager._scan_keys() helper: cursor-based SCAN, count=500

* ci: retrigger after hypha-rpc 0.21.31 published to PyPI

* fix: increase SCAN count from 500 to 10000 to prevent timeout under load

With count=500, get_service_info() required N/500 SCAN round-trips to find
a service key. Under 50 concurrent ASGI requests on a Redis instance with
thousands of accumulated keys, this caused get_remote_service() to exceed
the 10s timeout, returning 500 to HTTP clients.

count=10000 completes a typical scan in 1-2 round-trips while remaining
non-blocking, eliminating the race between scan latency and the RPC timeout.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: yield event loop in _get_workspace_manager to allow registration task to complete

With the SCAN count bumped to 10000, _scan_keys() now often finishes in a
single Redis round-trip.  With fakeredis (and fast real Redis), that
round-trip completes without ever suspending the coroutine, so the
asyncio event loop never gets a chance to run the register_client()
background task that was scheduled in RedisRPCConnection.on_message().

As a result, register_local_client() had not been called by the time
serve() was invoked, causing inbound RPC callbacks (receive/send) from
the service client to be routed via the non-local path.  Because
subscribe_to_client_events() also hadn't run yet, those messages were
lost and the 10 s _rintf_timeout fired, returning 500 for every
concurrent ASGI request.

Fix: add a single asyncio.sleep(0) before get_manager_service().  This
yields for exactly one event-loop iteration, which is enough for
register_local_client() (pure in-memory, no I/O) to complete for all
concurrently-created ephemeral client connections.  After that, the
LOCAL-ONLY delivery optimisation works correctly and no Redis pub/sub
subscription is needed for the fast path.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: explicitly await registration task in _get_workspace_manager

Replace asyncio.sleep(0) with an explicit await of the
_registration_task from RedisRPCConnection.  sleep(0) is not
guaranteed to give the task enough scheduler time on all platforms
(notably Linux CI where fakeredis SCAN completes without yielding),
while directly awaiting the task ensures register_local_client() is
done before get_manager_service() sends the first RPC message.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: wait only for local registration, not full psubscribe, before get_manager_service

The _registration_task does two things:
1. register_local_client() - adds client to _local_clients (needed for LOCAL-ONLY routing)
2. subscribe_to_client_events() - calls psubscribe() on shared _pubsub (slow under load)

Previously we awaited the full _registration_task, including the slow psubscribe()
call. Under CI load with 10 concurrent connections all calling psubscribe() on the
shared fakeredis pubsub, this caused timeouts in get_remote_service().

Fix: introduce _local_registration_done asyncio.Event set right after
register_local_client() completes. In _get_workspace_manager(), wait for this
event (which completes instantly) rather than the full _registration_task.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: register local client synchronously in on_message() to eliminate race condition

The previous approach using asyncio.create_task(register_client()) meant that
register_local_client() only ran when the event loop yielded.  Under CI load
with 10 concurrent connections, background psubscribe() tasks could saturate
the event loop, delaying _handle_method tasks beyond the 10s _rintf_timeout.

Root cause: The LOCAL-ONLY delivery path in RedisEventBus.emit() checks
_local_clients on every message.  If the ephemeral client-* isn't registered
yet, messages fall through to Redis pub/sub which requires psubscribe to be
active — a slow operation that caused routing failures under load.

Fix: Add register_local_client_sync() that performs the _local_clients.add()
immediately (synchronous, no IO).  Call it directly from on_message() so the
client is in _local_clients BEFORE any RPC message can possibly arrive.  The
background task now only handles subscribe_to_client_events() (psubscribe).

This eliminates the race condition without any await/sleep hacks.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: abort workspace unload when clients join between empty-check and unload

The `unload()` function performed a second `_list_client_keys()` check
after `unload_if_empty` already confirmed the workspace was empty. In a
race condition, clients that connected between the two checks were
force-disconnected via the `unload:{ws}` event, causing in-flight
requests to time out (test_asgi_concurrent_requests: assert 500 == 200).

Fix: add a `force` parameter to `unload()`. When `force=False` (the
default, used by `unload_if_empty`), if clients are found on the second
check, abort the unload rather than emitting `unload:{ws}`. Only explicit
admin calls (`force=True`) will force-disconnect remaining clients.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: add api.disconnect() to test_asgi to prevent stale service in Redis

Without explicit disconnect, the test-asgi service from test_asgi remains
in Redis after the test ends (circular refs prevent GC). This causes
test_asgi_concurrent_requests to receive serve() calls routed to the stale
client when using ?_mode=last, resulting in 30s timeouts.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: replace remaining redis.keys() calls with _scan_keys() in store.py

Replace two blocking redis.keys() calls in _cleanup_orphaned_client_services()
with non-blocking _scan_keys() (cursor-based SCAN). Fixes the
test_redis_store_has_no_redis_keys_calls assertion.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: allow get_logs for completed Python eval sessions after disconnect cleanup

Race condition: subprocess exits → event loop processes FIN during
process.communicate() await → client_disconnected → worker.stop() clears
_sessions → process.communicate() returns → _session_data populated.

Result: _sessions empty but _session_data has logs. get_logs fails with
SessionNotFoundError even though the logs are available.

Fix: check _session_data first; only raise SessionNotFoundError when
neither _sessions nor _session_data has the session.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: fall back to Redis session logs when worker session already cleaned up

Race condition in get_logs: client_disconnected fires between
_get_session_from_redis (which finds the session) and worker.get_logs
(which fails because stop() already cleared _sessions/_session_data).

Fall back to logs stored in the Redis session_data when the worker
raises any exception (e.g. SessionNotFoundError), since _store_session_in_redis
already persists the logs dict from python_eval before cleanup.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: preserve python-eval session on disconnect so logs remain retrievable

When a python-eval subprocess completes, it disconnects from the server,
triggering client_disconnected → worker.stop() which clears _session_data,
then _remove_session_from_redis which removes the Redis entry.

If get_logs is called after this cleanup (but before explicit stop()),
the session is not found anywhere → SessionNotFoundError.

Fix: for python-eval sessions, handle disconnect by updating status to
"stopped" in Redis with a 5-minute TTL instead of calling worker.stop()
and removing from Redis. This preserves both the Redis entry and the
worker's _session_data so get_logs can retrieve logs until explicit stop().

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: iterate over snapshot copy in EventBus.emit() to prevent once() from skipping subsequent handlers

Root cause: EventBus.emit() iterated directly over self._callbacks[event_name].
A once() handler removes itself via off() during execution, which mutates the
list mid-iteration. Python's list iterator then skips the element at the
now-shifted index, silently dropping every handler registered *after* the
once() handler.

Fix: iterate over list(self._callbacks.get(event_name, [])) — a snapshot copy
taken at loop-start. off() still modifies the original list (so once() still
only fires once), but the iteration is unaffected.

Affected scenarios:
  - once() then on(): the on() handler was silently dropped
  - two once() registrations: only the first fired

Tests added: test_event_bus_once_mutation.py (6 cases, pure fakeredis, no Docker)

* fix: cache python-eval logs in Redis at startup so get_logs() survives stop()

The python-eval subprocess completes synchronously inside worker.start().
Logs were stored only in the python_eval worker's in-memory _session_data,
never in the Redis session record.  After worker.stop() is called (e.g. by
a prior test that used the same app alias and cleaned up), _sessions and
_session_data are cleared.  A subsequent get_logs() call finds the session
in Redis, calls worker.get_logs() which raises SessionNotFoundError, and the
Redis-fallback path (redis_logs = session_data.get("logs")) returned None,
causing the exception to propagate and test_python_worker_integration to fail.

Fix: right after start_by_type() returns for python-eval apps, fetch logs
from the worker (while _session_data is still populated) and embed them in
the session_data dict before it is written to Redis.  The existing fallback
path in get_logs() can then serve logs from Redis even after stop() clears
worker memory.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: make test_websocket_connections_metric robust against concurrent test connections

The global websocket_connections gauge is shared with ALL tests running in the
same server instance.  When 15+ connections from previous tests are open, any
one of them may close while this test is connecting api1, causing the net count
to be unchanged (connect +1, concurrent cleanup -1 = 0 delta).

Replace exact absolute-count assertions with relative-delta assertions: snapshot
the gauge just before each connect/disconnect and verify the gauge moved in the
right direction (>=+1 or <=-1) within a 3-second polling window.  This tolerates
concurrent changes from other tests without losing meaningful coverage of the
gauge's behaviour.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: use monotonic counter for websocket connect assertions in observability test

The websocket_connections Gauge is shared across all concurrent tests and
can drop significantly (e.g. 13 -> 9) while we connect, making relative
+1 assertions unreliable.

Add websocket_connections_established Counter that only increments.
Use it for connect-side assertions (counter_after >= counter_before + 1
is always sound regardless of concurrent disconnections). Keep the Gauge
for disconnect assertions since a decrease is isolated to our own call.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* fix: address review issues — shared scan utility, version bumps, TTL throttle, TOCTOU, mock-free tests

- Add `scan_redis_keys()` shared utility in hypha/utils/__init__.py; replace
  triplicated `_scan_keys` impls in apps.py, store.py, workspace.py with
  delegation calls — eliminates count=500 vs count=10000 inconsistency and
  the copy-paste docstring error ("~N/500 round-trips" for count=10000)

- Bump helm-charts and docker-compose files to 0.21.71 (missed in original
  version bump commit — would cause helm deployments to pull the wrong image)

- Add docstring to `unload(force=False)` clarifying when force=True should be

- Throttle TTL sweep in `_update_load_metric` to run every 50 calls instead

- Fix `_do_idle_cleanup`: use fresh `time.time()` in TOCTOU re-check (stale

- Rewrite `test_ping_client_timeout.py` and `test_websocket_idle_toctou.py`
  without `unittest.mock`: replace MagicMock/AsyncMock with plain async

- Fix `test_client_metrics_ttl_expiry`: reset `_metrics_sweep_counter` to 49
  before invoking the metric update so the throttled sweep fires on first call

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* test: add TTL auto-deletion test and fix cleanup period for issue #919

- Add --s3-cleanup-period=2 to fastapi_server_sqlite fixture so TTL
  tests can run without Docker (default 300s made TTL testing impractical)
- Add test_s3_ttl_auto_deletion: verifies that files uploaded with TTL

Closes #919

Co-Authored-By: Claude Sonnet 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

* perf: deduplicate redundant SCAN in register_service() for issue #908

The singleton check and the peer-conflict check both scanned Redis with
the identical pattern `services:*|*:{workspace}/*:{service_name}@*`.
When registering a singleton service, this caused two full Redis SCAN
rounds over the same keyspace.

Lift the single scan before both checks so the result is shared.
Combined with the earlier KEYS→SCAN migration, register_service() now
issues at most 3 SCAN calls instead of the original 4 blocking KEYS.

Closes #908
